# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply (System Design One)

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903

**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20, originally from Neo Kim / System Design One (Substack #154)

**Authors:** Neo Kim and Paul Hoekstra
**Date:** June 17, 2026

Paul Hoekstra is a data engineer at Digital Power (data and AI consultancy), writes Paul's Pipeline newsletter.

## Summary

A comprehensive guide to agentic engineering concepts organized into 6 layers: Foundations, Configuration, Capability, Orchestration, Guardrails, and Observability. The thesis: learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves — the pace of new tooling is too fast to chase.

## The Six Layers

### Foundations
1. **What is an agent?** — An LLM that runs in a loop, has tool access, and decides what to do next. Produces chains of actions.
2. **[More content in full article]**

### Configuration
- Config files, reusable workflows, prompt caching, context rot limits

### Capability
- Live documentation, AI-native search, long-term memory, knowledge the agent queries but never owns

### Orchestration
- Coordinating multiple agents without conflict

### Guardrails
- Sandboxing, permissions, hooks, commit gates

### Observability
- Tracing, logging, replay, metrics

pages.title: Karpathy's LLM Wiki Gist
pages.body:
# Karpathy's LLM Wiki Gist

**Source:** email from sayfcodes@gmail.com  
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee3343b6e5b9e9  
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:15:00Z  
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf from his personal account to Maloha Coast info

[[Andrej Karpathy]] published a gist outlining a pattern for building **persistent, LLM-maintained knowledge wikis** — the opposite of traditional RAG where knowledge is re-derived on every query.

## Key Ideas

- **Persistent wiki** — instead of retrieving from raw docs at query time, an LLM incrementally builds and maintains an interlinked markdown wiki that sits between you and raw sources. Knowledge is compiled once and kept current.
- **Three-layer architecture:**
  1. **Raw sources** — immutable curated documents (articles, papers, data)
  2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown pages (summaries, entities, concepts, synthesis)
  3. **Schema** — a config file (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md) telling the LLM wiki structure and conventions
- **Operations:**
  - **Ingest** — drop sources, LLM reads, discusses, writes summary, updates index and all relevant entity pages
  - **Query** — LLM searches wiki, synthesizes answers with citations. Good answers become new wiki pages.
  - **Lint** — periodic health check: contradictions, stale claims, orphans, missing cross-references
- **Special files:** `index.md` (content catalog, updated on each ingest) and `log.md` (chronological record of operations)
- **Why it works:** LLMs handle the tedious maintenance burden that makes humans abandon wikis. Human curates sources and asks good questions; LLM does the bookkeeping.

### Historical Context

Karpathy relates this idea to [[Vannevar Bush]]'s Memex (1945) — a personal, curated knowledge store with associative trails between documents. The part Bush couldn't solve was who does the maintenance; the LLM handles that.

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

This is a direct validation of sayf's gbrain architecture — the exact pattern described here is already running in production with gbrain as the wiki layer and this Hermes pipeline as the LLM maintainer. Key resonances:
- gbrain pages = Karpathy's wiki layer
- `brain-ops` skill = Karpathy's schema (CLAUDE.md equivalent)
- Cron email ingestion = automated ingest
- The index/log pattern mirrors gbrain's metadata structure

## Link

Original gist: https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f

## Related

- [[Andrej Karpathy]] — authored the gist
- [[Vannevar Bush]] — historical precursor (Memex)
- [gbrain brain-ops skill](concepts/brain-ops)
- [gbrain architecture](concepts/gbrain-architecture)

# Maloha Coast — Brand Identity

## Finalised Brand Decisions (locked June 2026)

### Colour Palette
- **Primary**: #2a6a8a (teal blue)
- **Accent**: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- **Dark Navy**: #0a1628
- **Light backgrounds**: #f4f8fb, #ffffff

### Typography
- **Primary font**: Optima (humanist, elegant Roman forms)
- **Fallback stack**: 'Segoe UI', 'Candara', 'Palatino Linotype', 'Book Antiqua', Palatino, serif

### Brand Pattern
⬡ **Honeycomb Hexagon Grid** — Gold on Navy
- Hex lines: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- Background field: #0a1628 (dark navy)
- Tile size: 90×52 px seamless tiling PNG
- Hosted at: https://iili.io/CK3nHLF.png
- Also available in 7 other colour variants (see skill references)

## Email Templates

The production email template is based on the "Ocean Deep" direction (selected from 5 brand concepts A-E).

### Template Structure
1. **Outer wrapper**: Gold-on-navy hex pattern background (via HTML `background` attribute on `<td>`, NOT CSS)
2. **Header**: Pattern visible with rgba(10,22,40,0.65) semi-transparent overlay → "MALOHA COAST" in white + "Sdn Bhd" in gold
3. **Body card**: White rounded card with generous padding
4. **Info cards**: Gold left accent border (#c4a86a) on light teal (#f4f8fb) background
5. **Signature**: Gold horizontal rule (#c4a86a) + name/title/company/tagline in brand colours
6. **Footer**: Same hex pattern background as header

### Gmail Compatibility (verified)
- Use HTML `background` attribute on `<td>` elements for patterns (CSS `background-image` with data URIs is stripped by Gmail)
- Use nested `<table>` with `rgba()` overlay for colour tinting
- Use inline styles only (no `<style>` blocks)
- Max width 600px for email card
- No JavaScript

### Skill Reference
The full workflow is in the `html-email-with-sheet-logging` skill:
- **SKILL.md**: /home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/productivity/html-email-with-sheet-logging/SKILL.md
- **Production template**: references/ocean-deep-template.html
- **Header fragment**: templates/maloha-coast-header-fragment.html
- **Hex pattern variants**: references/hex-pattern-variants.md
- **Script to regenerate**: scripts/generate-hex-pattern.py

### Brand Concepts File
Full brand exploration HTML (5 palettes, 12 patterns, typography pairings):
/home/ubuntu/maloha-coast-brand-concepts.html

pages.title: Ai Data Center Architecture 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# AI Data Center Architecture — Why AI Breakthroughs Are Really Infrastructure Breakthroughs

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf from System Design One newsletter (Neo Kim), 2026-06-20

**Original:** System Design One #155 by [[Neo Kim]] and [[Ashish Prajapati]]
**Date:** 2026-06-19

---

> **Key insight:** The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models — they're coming from better infrastructure. AI data centers are factories, where GPUs are the factory floor machines and the network is the internal transport system.

---

## The Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory

- **AI Data Center = Factory**
- **Data = Raw material**
- **GPUs = Machines on the factory floor**
- **Network = Internal transport system**
- **Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials**
- **Software = Assembly line logic**
- **Output = Trained intelligence**

Remove any one ingredient and the entire system stalls.

---

## First Problem: Physics

Before AI is a software problem, it's a physics problem.

### 1. Power
A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kW — roughly 40 homes at full load. You're limited by how much electricity your facility can physically receive and distribute.

### 2. Cooling
More power = more heat. Modern data centers adopt **liquid cooling** — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs/CPUs, pumped to heat exchangers. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption, and consistent performance at scale.

### 3. Space
Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined, not just software.

---

## Compute: Why GPUs Took Over

- **CPU** = manager (a few powerful cores, complex sequential tasks, decision-making)
- **GPU** = factory workers (thousands of simpler cores, matrix multiplications in parallel)
- **DPU** = logistics (networking, storage I/O, security — keeps materials moving)

AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times. GPUs are purpose-built for this.

---

## Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

GPUs don't fail because they're slow. They fail because they're starved for data.

### Memory Hierarchy (speed vs capacity)

| Layer | Characteristics |
|-------|-----------------|
| GPU Memory (HBM) | Blazing fast but tiny (tens of GB) |
| CPU RAM | Hundreds of GB, slower than HBM |
| SSD/NVMe | Enormous capacity, slower still |
| Network/Object Storage | Nearly unlimited, slowest link |

### Tiered Storage in Practice

- **Hot:** NVMe SSDs — active training datasets, model checkpoints
- **Warm:** Parallel file systems (Lustre, GPFS) — high-speed shared access across many GPUs
- **Cold:** Object storage (Amazon S3) — raw datasets, older checkpoints

AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not just GPU compute speed.

---

## Networking: The Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

Thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate, sharing what they've learned at every step. Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is wasted investment.

### Cluster Topology

Leaf-spine network architectures — every GPU server reaches every other in at most 2 hops.

### Speed Hierarchy

- **NVLink** — fastest, a private highway between GPUs in the same server
- **InfiniBand/RoCE** — "bullet train" between separate servers
- **Ethernet** — general-purpose, not designed for AI's bandwidth/latency demands

### Amdahl's Law
Adding more GPUs increases computing power but also increases communication overhead. At a certain point, the bottleneck shifts from computation to communication.

### Smarter Data Movement Techniques

- **RDMA** — direct memory-to-memory transfers between servers, low-latency high-throughput
- **RDMA kernel bypass** — network adapter accesses application memory directly from user space
- **GPUDirect** — GPUs read/write to storage/network cards bypassing CPU entirely
- **Zero-copy pipelines** — data takes shortest path from source to GPU

The goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant unified processor.

---

## People Mentioned

- [[Neo Kim]] — Author of The System Design Newsletter
- [[Ashish Prajapati]] — AI infrastructure specialist, technology educator; NVIDIA-Certified Associate; created BeSA (Become a Solutions Architect) mentoring program reaching 23,000+ professionals; training programs reached 39,000+ learners worldwide

---

## Related Concepts

- [[AI Data Centers]]
- [[GPU Infrastructure]]
- [[Distributed Training]]
- [[RDMA]]
- [[Liquid Cooling]]

⬡ **Honeycomb Hexagon Grid** — Gold on Navy
- Hex lines: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- Background field: #0a1628 (dark navy)
- Tile size: 90×52 px seamless tiling PNG
- Hosted at: https://iili.io/CK3nHLF.png
- Also available in 7 other colour variants (see skill references)

## Email Templates

The production email template is based on the "Ocean Deep" direction (selected from 5 brand concepts A-E).

### Template Structure
1. **Outer wrapper**: Gold-on-navy hex pattern background (via HTML `background` attribute on `<td>`, NOT CSS)
2. **Header**: Pattern visible with rgba(10,22,40,0.65) semi-transparent overlay → "MALOHA COAST" in white + "Sdn Bhd" in gold
3. **Body card**: White rounded card with generous padding
4. **Info cards**: Gold left accent border (#c4a86a) on light teal (#f4f8fb) background
5. **Signature**: Gold horizontal rule (#c4a86a) + name/title/company/tagline in brand colours
6. **Footer**: Same hex pattern background as header

### Gmail Compatibility (verified)
- Use HTML `background` attribute on `<td>` elements for patterns (CSS `background-image` with data URIs is stripped by Gmail)
- Use nested `<table>` with `rgba()` overlay for colour tinting
- Use inline styles only (no `<style>` blocks)
- Max width 600px for email card
- No JavaScript

June 05, 2026 — AI Automation Society Newsletter

## The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

Sharper Claude context, Carson's first automation win, and Anthropic's $965B IPO filing

### In This Issue
- The single technique that makes every Claude project sound less like everyone else and more like you
- Carson landed his first automation client with a free pilot — here's the exact move
- Anthropic just filed for IPO at $965 billion — what it means if Claude is your primary tool

### New Video: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

If your Claude outputs feel generic — technically correct but missing your voice — the problem is not the model. Everyone using Claude Opus 4.8 starts from the same foundation. The difference between outputs that sound like you and ones that sound like everyone else is context.

**The Grill Me skill** fixes that. Instead of a five-minute brain dump before you start building, it interviews you until it actually understands how you think. It asks one question at a time, checkpoints your answers to a markdown file in your project, and keeps going until there are no gaps. By the end, you have reusable context your AI pulls from every time you touch that skill or project.

**The mistake most people make:** Starting with a brain dump and calling it good enough. Five minutes of info-dumping gets your output quality to about 70% on iteration one. The Grill Me approach gets you to 90% from the start — fewer revision cycles, faster time to a deliverable clients actually like.

**How this helps you:** When Claude knows your voice, your decisions, and your process, it stops generating generic output. Your projects feel like yours. Clients notice. You spend less time fixing what the AI got wrong.

### Winning Is All We Do — [[Carson]] landed his first automation client

**What happened:** [[Carson]] built a lead automation for a local aesthetics clinic as a free pilot. The workflow routes contact data and handles follow-ups automatically. He's waiting on Facebook credentials to go live, but the clinic owner is already highly receptive. His plan: deliver the pilot, then position for a paid monthly retainer.

**The opportunity:** Local service businesses (clinics, salons, studios) are some of the easiest first clients to land. They have real lead problems, a low technology bar, and if you deliver, they talk to other business owners. A free pilot with a clear upsell path is one of the cleanest ways to get your first paying automation client.

### In the News — [[Anthropic]] filed for IPO at $965 billion

**The news:** On June 1, 2026, [[Anthropic]] — the company behind Claude — confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a proposed initial public offering. Valuation: $965 billion, putting it ahead of [[OpenAI]] ($852 billion after their last funding round). [[Anthropic]] joins SpaceX and [[OpenAI]] in what analysts are already calling a race to go public — all three expected to list this year.

**How it affects automation:** When your primary AI tool's parent company goes public, two things typically follow: more capital to build better models (good for you), and quarterly earnings pressure that tends to push enterprise-first roadmaps and price adjustments upward. This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to build your skills and client base now, before the landscape shifts again.

### AIS Live — July 11-12, 2026

[[AI Automation Society]] hosts a two-day event where real practitioners walk through real client engagements on stage. Day 1: see the exact projects being sold right now and build one live. Day 2: exactly how to get clients, even if you're a beginner with no credibility yet. General Admission starts at $97 (early bird ends June 7).

pages.body:
**Context:** Response to a conference producer who asked for examples after a cold outreach. Crafted for LinkedIn DM — punchy, concrete, pilot-friendly.

## The Response

Thanks for asking. Here are some practical ways I think a Hermes agent could fit into your events workflow if we set one up on your laptop or VPS:

**Speaker sourcing:** You feed it your target criteria once, and it scans hundreds of profiles, past talks, and decks. It comes back with a shortlist plus solid briefing notes in about 15 minutes instead of 3–4 hours of manual digging.

**Post-session repurposing:** Right after a talk, you drop in the recording or transcript and it generates a full LinkedIn article, 3-5 social posts, a newsletter summary, and key highlights. Turns one session into 30+ usable pieces.

**Sponsor deliverables:** It pulls from your existing docs and data to create pre-event copy, staff briefs, lead reports, and post-event ROI summaries — cutting out a big chunk of the 20 hours per sponsor tier your team usually spends.

**Business brain / company memory:** I'm actually building one of these for myself right now. We can set up a "gbrain" where the agent learns your past events, speakers, sponsors, what worked and what didn't. Then you can just ask it things like "What sponsors performed best last year?" or "Give me the checklist for a 500-person event" and it pulls from your own history.

**Closer:** Which one sounds like it would save you the most time or headache right now? Happy to walk through any of them in more detail or hop on a quick call to map it to how you currently work.

## Notes

- Crafted to be realistic, specific, and pilot-friendly
- No AI jargon — pitched as a tool they control
- Each example maps to a known pain point for conference producers
- The gbrain example doubles as a subtle demo of the product in use

pages.body:
# Hermes Agent to ingest
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Tue, 9 Jun 2026 10:50:14 +0800
**Source:** email forward

Here's a compact PRD designed specifically for Hermes to execute against.

PRD: Company Memory OS (Phase 1)

## Author

Authored by [[Sayf Codes]].

## Vision

Build a Company Memory Operating System that transforms fragmented
communication into a searchable institutional memory.

Initial delivery is service-first, software-second.

The goal is not to sell software access.

The goal is to deliver the outcome:
"Never lose important decisions, context, relationships, or knowledge
again."

---

## Problem

Small businesses, consultants, agencies, and founders lose critical
knowledge because it is scattered across:

- Email
- Meeting transcripts
- Documents
- Chat conversations
- Notes

Knowledge becomes unrecoverable after weeks or months.

Current solutions focus on storage.

Users need retrieval and context.

---

## Target Customer

### Primary — Consultants

Characteristics:
- 20-100 clients
- High email volume
- Multiple projects running simultaneously
- Knowledge-intensive work
- Decisions buried in communications

### Secondary
- Agency owners
- Startup founders
- Fractional executives
- Professional services firms

---

## Core Value Proposition

Transform company communications into a searchable memory system.

**Input:** Emails, Meeting transcripts, Documents

**Output:** Searchable knowledge base, Decision history, Relationship intelligence, Project memory, AI-assisted retrieval

---

## Initial Offer

Productized Service — Not SaaS. Human-assisted onboarding.

### Knowledge Audit

**Customer provides:** Emails, Documents, Meeting notes

**System generates:** Executive summary, Project map, Key decisions, Action items, Stakeholder map, Recurring themes, Knowledge gaps

**Deliverable:** Interactive dashboard + report.

---

## Success Metric

Customer says: *"I found something valuable that I would not have found otherwise."*

---

## MVP Scope

### Ingestion
Support: Email forwarding, Gmail export, Meeting transcripts, Markdown documents, PDFs

### Processing
Extract: People, Companies, Projects, Decisions, Tasks, Dates, Relationships
Generate: Summaries, Tags, Backlinks, Knowledge graph connections

### Retrieval
User can ask:
- What was decided about Project X?
- What commitments were made to Client Y?
- Show all discussions involving Person Z.
- What unresolved action items exist?

### Dashboard
Views: Decision Timeline, Relationship Graph, Project Knowledge Base, Open Actions, Search (natural language)

---

## Go-To-Market

**Goal:** Acquire first 3 paying customers.

**Offer:** Free Knowledge Audit

**Target:** Existing network, Founders, Consultants, Agency owners

**Message:** *"I've built a system that turns emails, meetings, and documents into a searchable company memory. Looking for 3 companies to test it with."*

---

## Validation Questions

1. What was most valuable?
2. What surprised you?
3. What would you pay monthly to keep this updated?
4. Which feature would you miss most?
5. Would you recommend this to someone else?

---

## Pricing Experiments

| Stage | Service | Price |
|-------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Knowledge Audit (one-time) | RM300 - RM1000 |
| 2 | Continuous Memory Service (monthly) | RM300 - RM1000/month |
| 3 | SaaS | Only after 10+ paying customers |

---

## Strategic Principle

Do not optimize for software. Optimize for customer outcomes.

Use Hermes + GBrain internally as operational infrastructure. The platform emerges from repeated customer success, not from building features first.

---

## North Star

Become the memory layer for small organizations.

Long-term vision: Communication → Knowledge Graph → Institutional Memory → Organizational Intelligence

~772 tokens/page → ~$0.0001/page

## Projection

| Pages | Est. Tokens | Est. Cost |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| 6 (current) | 4.6K | $0.0006 |
| 50 | 38K | $0.005 |
| 100 | 77K | $0.01 |
| 1,000 | 772K | $0.10 |
| 10,000 | 7.7M | $1.00 |
| 100,000 | 77M | $10.00 |

## Key Insight

The real bottleneck is content curation (writing meaningful pages), not API pricing. Even embedding the full text of *War and Peace* (~775K tokens) would cost only ~$0.10.

## Verified

- 4,633 tokens × $0.00013/K = $0.00060229 ✓
- Cost scales linearly with token count
- No minimum or fixed fees on text-embedding-3-large

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

### Known gap
- .env.template is missing from the current backup (README references it but file was not generated). Needs to be added on next backup run.

pages.title: Karpathy Llm Wiki Pattern 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# Karpathy's LLM Wiki Pattern

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee3343b6e5b9e9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Shared by sayf on 2026-06-20, gist by Andrej Karpathy

## Link

https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f

## Summary

Andrej Karpathy's "LLM Wiki" describes a pattern for building personal knowledge bases using LLMs. Instead of using RAG (where an LLM re-discovers knowledge from raw documents on every query), the LLM incrementally builds and maintains a persistent wiki — a structured, interlinked collection of markdown files that sits between the user and the raw sources.

## Core Idea

Most people's experience with LLMs and documents looks like RAG: upload files, LLM retrieves chunks at query time. The LLM is rediscovering knowledge from scratch on every question. No accumulation.

Instead, the LLM builds a **persistent wiki** — a structured, interlinked collection of markdown files. When you add a new source, the LLM reads it, extracts key information, and integrates it into the existing wiki — updating entity pages, revising topic summaries, noting where new data contradicts old claims. The knowledge is compiled once and kept current, not re-derived on every query.

This mirrors the gbrain architecture used in this very ecosystem. Key insight: *the wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact.*

## Architecture — Three Layers

1. **Raw sources** — immutable source documents (articles, papers, etc.)
2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown files (summaries, entity pages, concept pages)
3. **The schema** — config document (like CLAUDE.md) telling the LLM how the wiki is structured

## Operations

- **Ingest** — Drop a new source, LLM reads it, writes summaries, updates index and entity pages
- **Query** — Ask questions against the wiki; good answers can be filed back as new pages
- **Lint** — Periodically health-check: contradictions, stale claims, orphans, missing cross-references

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

This is highly relevant — the gbrain knowledge base at Maloha Coast follows essentially this same pattern. Karpathy's framing validates the architecture: persistent wiki (gbrain pages), LLM-maintained cross-references (wikilinks), source provenance tracking. The key difference: gbrain uses embedding-based search for query while Karpathy suggests an index.md file approach at moderate scale.

## Related

- gbrain — similar persistent wiki architecture
- workspace-intake — ingestion pipeline that mirrors Karpathy's "ingest" operation
- second-brain concept — overlaps with the Claude second-brain levels from the AI Automation Society newsletter

pages.title: Knowledge Graph As Moat
pages.body:
# Knowledge Graph as Moat

**Synthesized:** 2026-06-09
**Sources:** Graphify video (Jack, YouTube), email-to-gbrain pipeline benchmarks, Company Memory OS PRD

## The Pattern

The same architectural pattern works across two different domains:

| Layer | Code Domain (Graphify) | Business Domain (Company Memory OS / gbrain) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ingest** | Clone GitHub repo | Forward email, upload transcript |
| **Parse** | Read code, cluster into modules | Extract entities, detect relationships |
| **Graph** | Files → modules → god nodes | People → companies → deals → decisions |
| **Query** | "What depends on X?" | "What was decided about Project X?" |
| **Dashboard** | Interactive circle graph | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| **Agent** | Claude Code + Hermes | Hermes + gbrain |
| **Compounding** | Every session builds context | Every email ingested enriches memory |

## Core Insight

Both systems solve the same fundamental problem: **"rereading is the tax."**

- In code: re-reading the repo every conversation costs tokens and produces shallower answers
- In business: re-searching emails/meetings every time you need context costs time and lets decisions fall through cracks

The knowledge graph IS the moat. Once a codebase or a business has been mapped:
- Answers come from summaries, not re-parsing
- Dependencies and blast radius are visible before making changes  
- Context compounds across sessions
- Switching costs increase dramatically — the graph IS the switching cost

## Why This Validates Company Memory OS

Jack's video pitches Graphify as worth paying for because it saves Claude Code users money and produces better results. The same value proposition applies to Company Memory OS:

- For a consultant with 50 clients: "What commitments did I make to Client Y?" answered in <500ms
- For an agency owner: "Show me all discussions about the Q2 budget" without digging through email
- For a founder: "Who did I meet at the June conference and what did we discuss?"

The architecture is proven. The pattern is validated. The question is execution and positioning.

## Key References

- [[media/videos/graphify-solves-claude-code-problem]] — Full video analysis with chapter index, quotes, and architecture breakdown
- [[concepts/company-memory-os-ph1-prd]] — Phase 1 PRD for the Company Memory OS productized service
- The email-to-gbrain pipeline (cron job `776c7bf32c77`) is the working MVP of Phase 1

## Client Pitch Analogy

"What Graphify does for code — instant understanding of any codebase without reading through it — we do for your business communications. Forward an email. Get a searchable memory. Never lose a decision again."

pages.title: Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem
pages.body:
# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

pages.title: Openclaw Anatomy
pages.body:
# Fwd: The Anatomy of OpenClaw
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Tue, 9 Jun 2026 00:11:45 +0800
**Source:** email forward

Forwarded from **[[Neo Kim]]** ([[System Design One]]) — Issue #151.

> **Understanding How OpenClaw Works**

OpenClaw is an open-source autonomous AI agent that runs on your machine, connects to messaging apps, and executes tasks directly — no manual copy-paste needed. It bridges the "thinking layer" (LLMs like Claude, GPT, Gemini) and the "act layer" (tools, APIs, files).

## What OpenClaw Offers

- **Gateway**: Always-on Node.js server (port 18789) coordinating messaging apps, LLM, tools, and memory. Runs as a background daemon — persistence is the foundation of scheduled jobs, proactive alerts, and multi-channel messaging.
- **Multi-channel messaging**: WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack — shared context and memory across all platforms.
- **Skills platform (ClawHub, 5,400+ capabilities)**: Plain Markdown files that define specific tasks (check GitHub PRs, send Slack messages).
- **Persistent memory**: Markdown files on disk — inspectable, version-controllable.
- **Proactive scheduling**: Heartbeat + cron jobs that act without being prompted.
- **Model-agnostic**: Claude, GPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, Grok, local via Ollama — mix and match.

## How It Differs

- ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini: browser-only, no access to email/scripts, no persistence.
- Siri/Google Assistant: closed products, no custom skills or self-hosting.
- LangChain/CrewAI/AutoGen: developer frameworks requiring Python code.
- Claude Code/Codex CLI: terminal tools for coding sessions — not 24/7.
- **OpenClaw runs 24/7** for everything, not just code.

## Architecture Layers

**Gateway** (Control Center): Orchestrates everything — authenticates senders, assembles context, passes to LLM, handles tool calls, routes replies. Sends command confirmation requests (e.g., `rm -rf ~/logs/*.log` needs approval). Serves Control UI at `http://localhost:18789` for real-time monitoring. Supports hot configuration reloads.

**Channel Layer**: Translation adapters per platform — Telegram (bot token + polling/webhook), WhatsApp (reverse-engineered web protocol, QR auth), Slack (Socket Mode, two token types), Discord (gateway WebSocket). Normalizes messages into internal format. Security policies (allowlists, pairing) apply uniformly.

**LLM Layer**: Assembles system prompt from agent personality + available skills + retrieved memory + conversation history. Calls the AI model. Tool calls execute and feed results back — loop continues until final response. Compaction triggers at context limit (summarizes history).

**Plugin System**: Almost everything non-core is a plugin — Telegram, GitHub, memory. Plugins hook into lifecycle events (before/after tool calls, messages, session start/end). Enables audit logging, rate limiting, custom approval flows without touching core.

**Memory System**: Three-layer approach — (1) short-term: session files on disk, loads recent context only; (2) long-term: all sessions indexed into local database, auto-searches before answering; (3) standing notes: Markdown files in `~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/` as permanent briefing documents.

**Long-running tasks**: Agent acknowledges task, works in background, messages when done. State written to disk, not held in memory. Cron jobs in isolated session mode for truly long-running work.

## Key Insights

- The Gateway runs on your Mac Mini or VPS — accessible via Tailscale from any device.
- Control UI shows every tool call, model response, and approval request in real time.
- Model can be changed at any time without reconfiguration.
- If a single tool call takes too long, Gateway times out — break large tasks into chunks.
- DeepResearch runs long sessions but stops when done — OpenClaw never stops.

*This was a preview of a subscriber-only post from Neo Kim's System Design Newsletter.*

pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

**Source:** Email (OpenRouter Team newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-19T22:14:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email, received 2026-06-19

Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. OpenRouter reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives users controls to filter routing on them.

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** An account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. Opting out means OpenRouter will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in the provider routing docs.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers on OpenRouter are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU in-region routing

For enterprise accounts, OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai` base URL.

pages.body:
# The 3-Folder System: Atlas → Projects → End Products (Head, Hands, Heart)

**Source:** YouTube — "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture"
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rl7rvHbDqGk
**Format:** Video transcript
**Ingested:** 2026-06-05

## Summary

The speaker discovered that disorganized AI outputs were a reflection of disorganized thinking. After months of building AI agents and burning API tokens, he stopped and realized the real problem wasn't the tools — it was his cognitive architecture. His core insight: **your file structure is a map of your thinking**. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar.

The solution is embarrassingly simple: a three-folder system that maps to Head, Hands, and Heart.

## The Three-Folder Framework

### 1. Atlas (Head / Thinking)
Raw materials, ideas, strategy, capture. Where you put ideas when you get them — the thinking layer. Everything that isn't yet a project.

### 2. Projects (Hands / Building)
Active work. Content being created, things being built. This is the doing layer.

### 3. End Products (Heart / Serving)
Completed work. Finished deliverables that serve other people. The output layer.

## Key Insights

- **File structure = cognitive architecture.** Your external files reveal your internal thinking patterns.
- **Organization is speed.** [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor): "Organization is heaven's first law." Clear structure makes AI faster and cheaper (fewer tokens).
- **The sophistication trap.** Smart people overcomplicate things. The answer is usually simpler than you think.
- **AI reveals you to yourself.** AI doesn't just do thinking FOR you — it shows you HOW you think by externalizing your patterns.
- **Agents mirror human growth.** An AI agent needs identity, context, and skills — the same three things a human needs to change their life.
- **Stop building, start organizing.** Before chasing the next tool or agent, fix the foundation.

## Claude Mirrors the Same Structure

| Mode | Purpose | Maps To |
|------|---------|---------|
| Chat | Thinking, exploring ideas | Atlas (Head) |
| Co-work | Building artifacts, plans | Projects (Hands) |
| Claude Code | Finished products, shipped work | End Products (Heart) |

## The Three Communities Pattern

The speaker's Skool communities also follow this structure:
- **Free community** (Lifestyle Founders Group) → Thinking, sharing ideas (Atlas)
- **Paid community** (Claude for Coaches) → Active building, workshops (Projects)
- **VIP community** → End products, completed work (Heart)

## Notable Quotes

- "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar."
- "Organization is heaven's first law." — [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor)
- "The bottleneck isn't the tool, it's the clarity of your thinking. Your cognitive architecture is now your competitive advantage."
- "Fix the inside, the outside follows. As above, so below."
- "I need a second brain sounds nice, but actually my first brain needs to get organized first."

## People/Resources Mentioned

- [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor) — "Organization is heaven's first law"
- [Jake Van Cleef](people/jake-van-cleef) — academic paper on stopping agent building, fixing file structure first
- [Nick Milo](people/nick-milo) — Obsidian/second brain advocate, three-folder patterns
- Head, Hands & Heart Notes: https://imported-abacus-8dd.notion.site/
- Free Skool Community: https://skool.com/lfg
- Claude For Coaches: https://www.skool.com/claudeforcoaches/about
- Lifestyle Founder Mastermind: https://www.skool.com/quantum100/about

pages.title: Virtual Machines Deep Dive
pages.body:
# How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive (#148: Part 1 - DevOps Mastermind)

**From:** [[Neo Kim]] (via systemdesignone@substack.com, forwarded by Sayf Codes)
**Date:** Mon, 25 May 2026
**Source:** email forward

---

**DevOps Mastermind** — A newsletter series to help engineers master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing commands. Architecture breakdowns of core DevOps systems, deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and practical insights into scalability, automation, and reliability.

---

## Emulation vs Virtualization

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware (e.g., retro gaming emulators). Each instruction from the old system is translated for the modern chip. It works, but it's slow.

Virtualization creates a version of the same hardware the machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor. Result: near-native speed.

- Emulation = hiring a translator for every sentence
- Virtualization = both people already speak the same language

This speed is what made virtualization practical at scale — you cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a single server. With virtualization, you can.

## What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A VM is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine. It's presented with CPU, memory, storage, and network — just like a physical system. The OS inside has no awareness it's sharing the machine.

**The hypervisor** is the layer that makes the illusion possible. Every request for CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network goes through it. Three key capabilities:

1. **Isolation** — each VM runs independently; a failure in one doesn't affect others
2. **Portability** — VMs can move between physical systems with minimal changes
3. **Efficient resource use** — multiple workloads share the same hardware

## Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion

### Type 1 vs Type 2

| Type | Description | Examples |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Type 1 (bare-metal)** | Runs directly on physical hardware, no host OS in between | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM |
| **Type 2 (hosted)** | Runs on top of an existing OS like a regular app | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

### Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

- **Full virtualization**: Guest OS runs unmodified; hypervisor handles all abstraction work
- **Paravirtualization**: Guest OS is aware it's virtualized and cooperates with the hypervisor to reduce overhead
- Trade-off: compatibility (full) vs performance (para)

### Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Intel VT-x and AMD-V let the CPU itself differentiate between VMs and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor automatically. This made virtualization practical at scale.

### Nested Virtualization

Running a VM inside another VM. Useful for testing and local Kubernetes setups but unsuitable for production due to overhead.

## CPU Virtualization

### The Privilege Model

In a normal system, the OS sits at the highest privilege level. In a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the OS. The guest OS gets moved down a level — but never knows it was demoted.

When the guest tries a privileged operation, the processor hands control to the hypervisor.

### vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a VM, you assign vCPUs (virtual CPUs). The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores via **time-slicing** — the same way an OS runs multiple apps.

**CPU overcommit**: Assigning more vCPUs than physical cores (e.g., 16 cores running 32 vCPUs). Works if all VMs aren't busy simultaneously. When demand rises, contention reduces performance.

### VM-exit and VM-entry

- **VM-exit**: VM tries a privileged instruction → processor stops it → control transfers to hypervisor
- **VM-entry**: Hypervisor handles the request → hands control back to the VM exactly where it left off
- Handoff takes microseconds. But frequent exits hurt performance.

### NUMA Awareness (Non-Uniform Memory Access)

Modern servers have multiple processors, each with local memory. Accessing memory on a different processor is slower. The hypervisor tries to keep a VM's vCPUs and memory on the same NUMA node.

For most small deployments this doesn't matter. For high-performance databases on large servers, it matters a lot.

---

|*This was a free preview of a subscriber-only post from [[Neo Kim]]'s "The System Design Newsletter". Guest author: [[Ayaan]] (self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure).*

## Key Takeaways

- Virtualization enabled cloud computing by splitting one powerful machine into many isolated ones
- The hypervisor is the core abstraction layer — handles CPU, memory, storage, and networking
- Hardware support (VT-x/AMD-V) made virtualization practical at scale
- Containers are a different approach (OS-level virtualization, shared kernel) — covered in Part 1's comparison

## People Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — Author of The System Design Newsletter (systemdesignone.substack.com). Wrote this deep dive on virtualization architecture as part of the DevOps Mastermind series.
- **[[Ayaan]]** — Self-taught DevOps engineer, guest author. Focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure. Builds in public on Twitter/X and GitHub.

# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

Zaim here. Wanted to capture this before I forget.

Between KSI sessions I have these 15-minute gaps. Started using them to jot down one thing I noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that I'd never have spotted otherwise.

3. Mid-career — all variations of 'is this it?'

The 15-minute rule: never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered
(and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.
The annual goal trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on.

"Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing
happens.

By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything
that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for
next year.

I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I
set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine
on paper.

Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week
Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They
operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline
is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

Btw, this book is available on Shopee!

I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March.

I hit it!!

The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I
built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.
Why this works for your career too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot.

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target
department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to
leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One of these is a wish. The other is a plan.

I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal
like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached,
mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year"
mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it.
How to set yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Follow this format and write down:

   - *My 90-day career goal is...*
   - *This matters to me because...*
   - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...*
   (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
   - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the
   outcome)

Your homework this weekend

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey
area.

Then build it into your week, not your year.
------------------------------

P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3
2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in
June. For more info or to sign up, head over to
https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6

# The 15-Minute Gap Method

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca04daacc7a09
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to Zaim's workspace via self-BCC

Zaim here. Wanted to capture this before I forget.

Between KSI sessions I have these 15-minute gaps. Started using them to jot down one thing I noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that I'd never have spotted otherwise.

Three buckets emerged:
1. Career pivoters — stuck on 'what if I fail' not 'what do I want'
2. Fresh grads — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory
3. Mid-career — all variations of 'is this it?'

The 15-minute rule: never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

— Zaim

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca58bcdb09c70

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

pages.body:
## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

### Known gap

pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** OpenRouter Team (newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

[Provider policy table](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging)

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via [data policy filtering](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing#requiring-providers-to-comply-with-data-policies).
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers — including Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, and Together — are zero retention. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

## If you need stricter guarantees

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`. [Contact enterprise team](https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form) to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

pages.title: Openrouter Three Features 2026 06 18
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on OpenRouter

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:30:00Z

OpenRouter onboarding email covering three features that are single-parameter changes with production value.

## Key Points

1. **Streaming** (`stream=True`): Tokens arrive as generated. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

2. **Structured outputs** (`response_format` with JSON schema): OpenRouter enforces JSON schema at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops for malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format; router respects it with `openrouter/auto`.

3. **Provider preferences** (`provider` param in `extra_body`): Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. Sort by `"price"` to always pick the cheapest, or use an allowlist to lock routing to trusted providers.

## Combined Example

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}],
)
```

## Useful Pages

- [Models & pricing](https://openrouter.ai/models)
- [Rankings (real usage volume)](https://openrouter.ai/rankings)
- [Usage & costs](https://openrouter.ai/activity)

pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-18
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0

## Summary

Three [[OpenRouter]] features that are each a single parameter change, don't require a new integration or endpoint, and each earns its place in production code.

## Key Features

### 1. Streaming

Set `stream=True` and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time — the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

### 2. Structured Outputs

Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` and [[OpenRouter]] enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the parameter.

### 3. Provider Preferences

Use the `provider` parameter to control which providers handle a request: set `ignore=`` to skip specific providers, set `order` to prioritize by latency or cost, or set `allow_fallbacks` to keep the request reliable even when preferred providers are down.

pages.title: Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem
pages.body:
# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

## Chapter Index

- 00:00 — Chapter 1: Graphify Solves Claude's Problem (hook + promise)
- 00:46 — Chapter 2: What Graphify Actually Does (the map metaphor)
- 01:21 — Chapter 3: How The Map Works (clustering, god nodes, facts vs guesses)
- 02:10 — Chapter 4: Why Maps Save Tokens (orientation, blast radius, re-reading tax)
- 03:17 — Chapter 5: Install And Set Up (clone repo, Claude Code integration)
- 04:38 — Chapter 6: See The Knowledge Graph (interactive visualization)
- 05:04 — Chapter 7: Query Any Repo Live (prompt example, cost comparison)
- 05:43 — Chapter 8: Take It Further (agentic operating system intro)
- 06:07 — Chapter 9: Inside The Operating System (dashboard, Hermes integration)
- 07:32 — Chapter 10: Import Any GitHub Repo (one-click ingestion)
- 08:07 — Chapter 11: Chat To Your Projects (conversational interface)
- 09:04 — Chapter 12: Connect Everything Together (shared brain architecture)
- 10:04 — Chapter 13: Visual Interface Wins (closing argument)

---

# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter newsletter to info@malohacoast.com

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — Account-wide toggle in privacy settings for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in provider routing docs.
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU Routing

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the https://eu.openrouter.ai base URL.

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Paul Hoekstra)
**Published:** Jun 17, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:04:29+08:00

#154: What an agent is, how to configure one, when to use multiple, and how to keep them from doing damage.

*Guest author: Paul Hoekstra — data engineer at Digital Power, data and AI consultancy; writes Paul's Pipeline.*

## Foundations

### 1. What is an agent?
An agent is an LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Instead of a single answer to a prompt, the model produces a chain of actions, each building on the last.

Use an agent when: task is open-ended, the next step depends on the last, you cannot script the path in advance. Skip the agent when a single prompt or plain script will do.

### 2. Execution model (Think-Act-Observe / ReAct)
Three steps, repeated: (1) model thinks — reads current state and decides next action; (2) acts — emits a tool call, harness validates and runs it; (3) observes — tool result joins conversation as context. The loop replaces upfront prediction with feedback, allowing the agent to recover from its own mistakes.

Two variations: parallelism (multiple tool calls in one act step) and blocking vs non-blocking (sync vs async).

### 3. Agent state
Two senses: (1) workflow progress — which step is the agent on; (2) everything the agent knows at any moment. Breaks into: context window (everything the model sees, hard ceiling) and everything outside it (files, DB, memory it fetches on demand). Files are the right default — diffable, versionable, easy to edit. Subagents get a fresh context window, parent passes only what's needed.

### 4. Common agent patterns
- **Planner/executor**: one plans, another executes. Keeps each agent in a suitable context shape.
- **Router/specialist**: router dispatches to specialists with tighter prompts and smaller toolsets. Cheaper, easier to reason about.
- **Map-reduce parallelism**: fan-out across subagents, merge results. Cost bounded by longest subagent.
- These combine in real workflows: planner spawns router → router fans out map-reduce → merged result comes back.

## Configuration Layer

### 5. Agent config files
Project-level Markdown loaded at session start (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md). The agent reads hard rules before writing code. Useful configs are short and specific: hard limits, actual tooling stack, behavioral rules. Keep under 100 lines.

### 6. Reusable workflow files (skills)
Markdown with YAML frontmatter — name, description, optional globs. Loaded on demand. SkillsBench showed Claude Haiku at 27.7% with human-curated skills beats Opus at 22.0% without them. Model-written skills saw gains disappear — generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse.

### 7. Workflow frameworks
Chains of skills, hooks, and slash commands wired into repeatable processes. Examples: Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering. Shared thread: understand what you're building, tell it how to work, check the result.

### 8. Prompt caching
Stores the stable prefix so the model doesn't re-read it every turn. First call pays a small premium to write the cache; later turns read at a discount. Cache TTL ranges from minutes to an hour depending on provider. Without caching you'd think carefully about config file size; with it, the cost is a rounding error.

### 9. Context rot
Model performance drops as context fills up. GPT-5.5: 98.1% on short documents → 74.0% at 1M tokens. Claude Opus 4.7: 59.2% at 256K → 32.2% at 1M. The model has to look past each token to focus on what matters.

## Key Insights
- Learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves — the pace of change won't slow down.
- Skills/benchmark data proves that good instructions beat a better model.
- Context rot is the hard constraint that all agentic workflows must design around.

pages.title: The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** email (sayf forward)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:15:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

**From:** Neo Kim, System Design One (#155)
**Original Date:** Fri, 19 Jun 2026
**Forwarded by:** Sayf → info@malohacoast.com

👋 Neo here! A free preview of a subscriber-only post on AI infrastructure.

## Core Thesis

The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models — they're coming from better infrastructure. AI data centers are factories, not server rooms.

## Mental Model: AI Data Center as Factory

| Component | Analogy |
|-----------|---------|
| AI Data Center | Factory |
| Data | Raw material |
| GPUs | Machines on the factory floor |
| Network | Internal transport system |
| Storage | Warehouse |
| Software | Assembly line logic |
| Output | Trained intelligence |

## Physical Constraints

1. **Power** — A single GPU rack draws 80-120 kW (≈40 homes). Limited by what the facility can physically receive.
2. **Cooling** — More power = more heat. Liquid cooling via cold plates attached directly to GPUs is the modern approach.
3. **Space** — Scaling AI requires real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics management.

## Compute Architecture

- **CPU** = Manager (decision-making, few powerful cores)
- **GPU** = Factory workers (thousands of simpler cores, mass production of matrix multiplications)
- **DPU** = Logistics (networking, storage I/O, security)

## Memory & Storage Bottleneck

GPUs are constantly starved for data. Memory hierarchy:
- GPU Memory (HBM) — blazing fast but tiny (tens of GB)
- CPU RAM — hundreds of GB
- SSD/NVMe — enormous capacity
- Object storage — nearly unlimited but slowest

Tiered storage: Hot (NVMe SSDs) → Warm (Lustre, GPFS) → Cold (Amazon S3)

## Networking

Leaf-spine architecture: every GPU server reaches every other in ≤2 hops.
- NVLink = intra-node (private highway within server)
- InfiniBand/RoCE = inter-node (bullet train between servers)
- Ethernet = general-purpose (not designed for AI workloads)

**Amdahl's Law:** Adding more GPUs eventually stops helping due to communication overhead.

**Smarter data movement:** RDMA (direct memory-to-memory), GPUDirect (bypass CPU), zero-copy pipelines.

## Key People

- **Neo Kim** — Author of System Design One
- **Ashish Prajapati** — AI infrastructure specialist, co-author of this post

## Related

# AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

**Source:** email from Sayf (forwarded)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622

**Original Author:** [[Tonbi]] (AI Garage)
**Original Publication Date:** 2026-06-19

> Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that open weights models can approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier.

## Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

[[Tonbi]]'s wife was using Claude in Excel, and he wondered if he could do it with [[Hermes Agent]]. After a false start with an overly complicated method, he found a simpler approach. The end result: a chat window inside Excel plus custom functions with Hermes. The video didn't go viral but got nice comments. He plans more integrations like this.

[How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR4-tDkO_ZYupC2QTCbId7IkHkiJxZzzIEuleA0Zxl07q_mIlFIjX1d-qo0fTV8ktpwzR6AzV7NOC9JaoERjTgGRKKpgQr8t9M6Ax6eRdxc2VS1KSZt9LH0au-x505YOJCIHODQTRnFuG4RF0Fca33iwrdB49JxH-Wpzn-QIBG9tCOiNcW0-lZMJIx8ziuwfUR4WLoyIC_B8CQQdw3BksEQ74jMfWREhLH2w5wWjK9POqQ/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h2/h001.Uo1z3BXiQOSpNs0ZoYyWZCZTVSRrzmTQWvnIe6dPxT8)

## AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** [[Anthropic]] removed Fable 5 access due to a new US government directive. [[Tonbi]] wasn't surprised — predicted this direction. Thinks it's some weird Anthropic/admin negotiating in public and will get cleared up eventually, but highlights the need for local and decentralized AI.

**GLM-5.2 released.** Open weights model. [[Tonbi]] gave it tasks in a first look video and has been using it. Feels "only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind." Incredible for an open source model, though most people won't be able to run it locally without serious hardware.

## Research & Experiments

**DR-DCI (Dynamic Retrieval-Direct Corpus Interaction).** Paper at arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885. Describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern combining RAG recall with filesystem agency. Key insight: hide huge corpus behind a retriever, let agent call `pull(query, topK)` to bring relevant docs into a local workspace, then use normal file tools. Increases accuracy while improving wall time and costs. [[Tonbi]] sees clear applications for [[Agent Wikis]].

## Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**[[Agent Wikis]]:** First release day got nearly 10k visitors (humans and agents), now doing 5k-7k daily. [[Tonbi]] created a loop for missed topics to be autonomously researched and added to wikis.
