pages.title: Ai Garage Weekly June 19 2026
pages.body:
# AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

**Source:** email forward from Sayf Codes to Maloha Coast
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T02:10:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf Codes on 2026-06-20

*Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI*

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really explode.

---

## 🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

So my wife was talking about using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that with Hermes Agent? I hadn't heard of that type of integration, I wasn't even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and asked my agent: Isn't there an easier way to do this? And after some extra research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH" I screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice comments on it, it didn't go super viral as I had hoped (lol).

Video: [How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=...)

---

## 📡 AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** Last Friday the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I was disappointed too, but I've been convinced this was the direction we were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn't going to let private companies and individuals have power like that. They'll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and decentralized AI moving forward. You can't let all that power be handed to only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have access to these tools.

**GLM-5.2 released.** I've been impressed with GLM models before, so when I heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video and I've been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think. Now of course most people won't be able to run them locally, it would take some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn't super slow and quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future.

---

## 📄 Research & Experiments

**Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion** (arXiv:2606.14885)

With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I've been researching is retrieval, how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus.

With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then answer from those snippets. With DCI, an earlier version of this method, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as well as significantly improved wall time and costs.

The applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear. I experimented with RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing project work.

---

## 💭 Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**Agent Wikis:** Nice release for Agent Wikis, people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we've been doing 5k-7k since then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I've been refining the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me).

I've been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the product. I think once I'm ready to do premium wikis with the stripe integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material (so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones the ads based on that. I've heard of people doing this, but that would be an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so I'll need the site to be profitable before I try that.

pages.body:
# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20, originally from Nate Herk (AI Automation Society)

## Summary

A breakdown of five levels of building a Claude second brain, a client email pipeline automation case study, and Codex hitting 5M weekly users with banked rate limit resets.

## In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall.
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up.
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, and there's a free bonus available through June 24.

## New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them isn't the tool. It's how the data is organized.

*The insight that changes how you build:* Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

*How this helps you:* Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

## Winning Is All We Do

Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch.

*What happened:* Robert's client had a call center calling leads, but nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that handles the rest: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts the email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign. No manual cleanup. No copy-paste.

*The opportunity for you:* Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

## In the News

Codex just hit 5 million weekly users. If you use it, there's a free bonus through June 24.

*The news:* OpenAI Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features alongside the milestone. One is permanent: banked rate limit resets. Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer, you can now save resets and use them when you actually need them. The other runs through June 24: Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

*How it affects you:* If you've been hitting Codex rate limits at the wrong moment mid-build, the banked reset system fixes that. You decide when to use your reset, not the clock. If you're not hitting limits yet, the referral is easy headroom to bank before the window closes. The bigger signal here: Codex is now growing through referral mechanics at 5 million weekly users.

# The Maloha Coast Navigator

*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** OpenRouter Team (newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

[Provider policy table](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging)

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via [data policy filtering](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing#requiring-providers-to-comply-with-data-policies).
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers — including Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, and Together — are zero retention. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

## If you need stricter guarantees

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`. [Contact enterprise team](https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form) to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

pages.body:
# Chain models for complex tasks

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed0c0b31514494
**Ingested:** 2026-06-16
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering chain models for complex tasks.

## Key Content

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. One endpoint handles both.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. Same endpoint, same auth, different model parameter. That is the point of having 300+ models on one API: you mix them per step instead of committing to one for the whole workload.

Browse models (  )

 

PATTERN 1: CLASSIFY, THEN GENERATE

# Step 1: classify with a fast, cheap model
classify = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {query}"}], )


# Step 2: generate with a strong model
result = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{classify.choices[0].message.content}: {query}"}], )


GPT-4o-mini runs around $0.15 per million input tokens. Claude Sonnet 4.6 runs around $3 per million. Using the cheap model for routing and the expensive one only where it earns its cost can cut total spend 5 to 10x on mixed workloads.

 

PATTERN 2: EXTRACT, THEN WRITE

# Step 1: structured extraction
extracted = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  response_format={"type": "json_object"},
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract fields as JSON: {doc}"}], )


# Step 2: long-form writing from the structured fields
draft = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a report from: {extracted.choices[0].message.content}"}], )


Structured outputs are cheap and fast on small models. Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short.

Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went.

**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team welcome email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-04

OpenRouter onboarding email — fastest path from signup to a working API response.

## Quickstart

Two code blocks (Python SDK or curl). Swap API key, run.

**Python (OpenAI SDK):**
```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    api_key="YOUR_API_KEY"
)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

**cURL:**
```bash
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openai/gpt-4o-mini","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}'
```

## Error Codes

- **401** — almost always the API key header
- **402** — free-tier daily cap hit or credits needed for paid model
- **404** — model slug format: OpenRouter uses dots, so `claude-sonnet-4.6` not `claude-sonnet-4-6`

## Free Tier

10 requests per day across 29 free models. No credit card required.

## Auto Routing

`openrouter/auto` picks models per-request based on the prompt. ~63% of auto traffic routes to models under $1/M tokens — simple tasks stop hitting expensive models without any routing code.

# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-19
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f

## Summary

[[OpenRouter]] provides structured data about each provider's retention and training policies, publishes a full table in their docs, and gives users three layers of control over which providers can process their prompts.

## Key Content

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint and publishes the full table in the docs.

### What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** An account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, [[OpenRouter]] will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override your account default on any single call via data policy filtering in the provider routing docs.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

### Enterprise Options

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`.

- Privacy settings: https://openrouter.ai/settings/privacy

# Three levers that lower your per-token cost

**Source:** email

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three levers that lower your per-token cost.

## Key Content

Tiered pricing, provider preferences, and prompt caching. All per-request, no extra wiring.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three things quietly cut your per-token cost on OpenRouter. They stack, and none of them require changing models or rewriting your app.

Compare model pricing (  )

 

1. TIERED PRICING

Most models run on tiered pricing set by the provider: the more tokens you send through, the less each token costs. You do not opt in. The discount applies automatically once you cross the threshold. Open any model page on openrouter.ai/models (  ) and click the Effective Pricing tab to see the live per-provider rate, including any tiers you have already hit. Consolidating traffic onto one or two models unlocks the next tier faster than scattering tokens across five.

2. PROVIDER PREFERENCES

The same model is served by multiple providers at different rates. provider in the request body lets you prefer cheaper providers, sort by price, or set a price ceiling. It is a per-request setting, so you can apply it selectively to traffic where latency matters less than cost. Docs: Provider Routing (  ).

3. PROMPT CACHING

On supported models, OpenRouter reuses cached prompt prefixes across requests, so repeated context costs a fraction of the first call. Useful for long system prompts, RAG, agents that replay history, and anything that ships the same context every turn. Per-request, no extra wiring. Docs: Prompt Caching (  ).

## Summary

Three OpenRouter features accessible via single parameter changes, no new integration needed.

### 1. Streaming
`stream=True` — tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first token. Agents that stream partial results to frontend get a noticeable UX lift.

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},

)

# Three features that quietly save you money

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ebc285fe5e7458
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three features that quietly save you money.

## Key Content

Prompt caching cuts repeat tokens by up to 90%. Fallback keeps you up. Healing fixes broken JSON.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three OpenRouter features quietly do work for you in production. They are off by default on most platforms and on or one-flag-away here. Each one pays for itself within the first week on any workload that hits real traffic.

View the docs (  )

 

PROMPT CACHING

If you send the same system prompt or context window repeatedly, OpenRouter caches the prefix and bills cached tokens at up to 90 percent off. It runs automatically on supported models with no setup. The savings show up in your usage report. Agents with long system prompts, RAG pipelines that reuse context, and multi-turn chat all benefit here without any code change.

 

PROVIDER FALLBACK

Pass a list of models with route="fallback" and OpenRouter retries the next one in the list when your primary fails. Same response shape, same cost as whichever model serves the request. Provider outages stop cascading to your app, and the only thing you added was one extra array entry.

 

RESPONSE HEALING

When a model returns malformed JSON, OpenRouter repairs it before sending the response back to your app. Enable it under Settings > Plugins (  ). Useful for any pipeline that parses model output downstream, which in practice is most production workloads.

 

client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
  extra_body={
    "models": ["openrouter/auto", "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6"],
    "route": "fallback"
  },
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}] )


 

Auto-routing with a named fallback. The production pattern.

Why these three compound. Auto-routing picks a cheap model when the prompt does not need a frontier one. Prompt caching drops the per-call cost of whatever the router picks on repeat traffic. Provider fallback keeps you up when a backend misbehaves. Layered together, the cost curve stays flat as traffic grows and the uptime profile looks nothing like single-provider integration.

The next email walks through structured outputs: how to get valid JSON from any model on the catalog with one parameter.

pages.title: Secret Architecture Behind Ai Data Centers 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Ashish Prajapati)
**Published:** Jun 19, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T07:35:22+08:00

#155: Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs

*Guest author: Ashish Prajapati — AI infrastructure specialist, technology educator. Training programs have reached 39,000+ learners. Free mentoring initiative "Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)" has helped 23,000+ professionals. NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and Operations (NCA-AIIO) bootcamp instructor.*

## Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls.

## First Problem: Physics

### 1. Power
A single GPU server rack draws 80-120 kW — roughly equivalent to 40 homes at full load. Limited by how much electricity the facility can physically receive and distribute.

### 2. Cooling
More power = more heat. Without removal: hardware fails, performance throttles, systems shut down. Modern data centers use **liquid cooling** — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption, consistent performance.

### 3. Space
Unlimited chips and power still need racks and floor space. Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

Takeaway: A data center is a power plant combined with a massive, precisely controlled air-conditioning system.

## Compute: Why GPUs Took Over
- **CPU** = manager (decision-making) — a few powerful cores, handles complex sequential tasks
- **GPU** = factory workers (mass production) — thousands of smaller cores, designed for parallel matrix multiplications
- **DPU** (Data Processing Unit) = logistics and operations — handles networking, storage I/O, security so CPU and GPU aren't interrupted

## Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage
GPUs don't fail because they're slow. They fail because they're hungry — constantly starved for data.

Memory hierarchy: GPU Memory (HBM — fastest, tiny, tens of GB) → CPU RAM (hundreds of GB, slower) → SSD/NVMe (enormous) → Network/Object Storage (nearly unlimited, slowest).

**AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not by how fast the GPU computes.**

Tiered storage: hot (NVMe SSDs for active datasets/checkpoints), warm (parallel file systems like Lustre/GPFS for shared access), cold (object storage like S3 for raw datasets).

## Networking: Hidden Bottleneck
When training large models with thousands of GPUs, they must constantly share what they've learned. Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is wasted investment.

- **Leaf-spine architecture**: every server can reach every other server in at most two hops
- **NVLink**: intra-node fabric, fastest path, direct GPU-to-GPU within a server
- **InfiniBand/RoCE**: inter-node fabric, "bullet train" between servers
- **Ethernet**: general-purpose, not designed for AI's extreme bandwidth/latency demands

**Amdahl's Law**: parts of the system must wait — adding more workers eventually stops helping. More GPUs = more computing power but also more communication overhead.

Smarter data movement: RDMA (low-latency direct memory transfers between servers), RDMA kernel bypass, GPUDirect (GPUs read/write to storage/network bypassing CPU), zero-copy pipelines.

Goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant, unified processor.

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes One Afternoon
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb960b3c2b3ec2
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12T09:09:30Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-12

---

Sayf's note: newsletter shared

---

**From:** [[Adrian (JS Mastery)]] | [[JS Mastery]]
**Subject:** Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.
**Date:** Thu, 11 Jun 2026

February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing. This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified. Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge. Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
[[Adrian (JS Mastery)]]
[[JS Mastery]]

pages.title: Zaim Career Goals 2026 06 16
pages.body:
# Why Your 2026 Career Goals Are Already Dead

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ece778ab23e039
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:50:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Zaim Mohzani via info+zaim@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-16

> Forwarded by [[Zaim Mohzani]] from Nation Building School newsletter.

It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered (and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.

## The Annual Goal Trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for next year.

[[Zaim Mohzani]] went through this with weight, not career. In January 2025, set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Then came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years — they operate in the last few weeks before a deadline. The fix is to shrink the deadline.

Rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March. Hit it. The habits built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.

## Why This Works for Your Career Too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot. "Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday. "By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday. One is a wish. The other is a plan.

Zaim has worked with clients on exactly this. Those who set a 90-day goal like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached mostly hit it or get close. Those with "find a better job this year" mostly don't, because there's no Monday task.

## How to Set Yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one. Follow this format:

- My 90-day career goal is...
- This matters to me because...
- To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have... (make it checkable — "3 offers" is checkable; "more clarity" is not)
- To make sure I complete it, I'm going to... (weekly actions, not the outcome)

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey area. Then build it into your week, not your year.

**P.S.** Zaim is running a free workshop on setting this 90-day goal for Q3 2026. Early next month. Sign up: https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6

## Key Ideas

- The annual goal trap: a year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on
- Shrink the deadline: 90-day goals with checkable outcomes create Monday-level actions
- Habits outlast goals — the 90-day sprint builds systems that persist
- Format: one goal, checkable outcome, weekly actions

## People Mentioned

- [[Zaim Mohzani]] — career coach, Nation Building School
- Brian Moran — co-author, "The 12 Week Year"
- Michael Lennington — co-author, "The 12 Week Year"

---

## Compilation of Facts (Compiled Truth)

Zaim Mohzani is a Malaysian career strategist, ASEAN policy specialist, and social entrepreneur. He helps mid-career professionals change careers without starting over, using a lifestyle-centric career design approach. He has directly helped 2,600+ professionals in Malaysia since 2015. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

He is Malaysia's first certified Designing Your Life (DYL) coach, trained by Bill Burnett and Dave Evans at Stanford University. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### Core Philosophy: Lifestyle-Centric Career Design

Zaim's approach rejects the "follow your passion" advice. Instead:
- Start with the lifestyle you actually want
- Work backwards to careers that support it
- Build skills with market value
- Make moves based on reality, not feelings

His key thesis: **Passion is a by-product of doing good work, not a prerequisite.** [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

### Personal Story

At 25, Zaim had strong credentials but was burnt out — hundreds of applications, zero offers. He was told "you just haven't found your passion yet." That advice kept him paralysed. He pivoted through five industries and at 28 became chairman of a Malaysian government agency — not because he found his calling, but because he stopped chasing passion and started designing for the life he wanted. [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

---

## Organizations Founded / Led

Social enterprise started as a bootstrapped NGO in Bukit Tunku, now empowering next-generation leaders. Has engaged over 10,000 youths in Malaysia and Southeast Asia. Focus: career workshops and leadership programmes for urban poor, single mothers, and youth at risk. During the pandemic, launched virtual career workshops. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

pages.title: Cold Lead Response Conference Events
pages.body:
**Context:** Response to a conference producer who asked for examples after a cold outreach. Crafted for LinkedIn DM — punchy, concrete, pilot-friendly.

## The Response

Thanks for asking. Here are some practical ways I think a Hermes agent could fit into your events workflow if we set one up on your laptop or VPS:

**Speaker sourcing:** You feed it your target criteria once, and it scans hundreds of profiles, past talks, and decks. It comes back with a shortlist plus solid briefing notes in about 15 minutes instead of 3–4 hours of manual digging.

**Post-session repurposing:** Right after a talk, you drop in the recording or transcript and it generates a full LinkedIn article, 3-5 social posts, a newsletter summary, and key highlights. Turns one session into 30+ usable pieces.

**Sponsor deliverables:** It pulls from your existing docs and data to create pre-event copy, staff briefs, lead reports, and post-event ROI summaries — cutting out a big chunk of the 20 hours per sponsor tier your team usually spends.

**Business brain / company memory:** I'm actually building one of these for myself right now. We can set up a "gbrain" where the agent learns your past events, speakers, sponsors, what worked and what didn't. Then you can just ask it things like "What sponsors performed best last year?" or "Give me the checklist for a 500-person event" and it pulls from your own history.

**Closer:** Which one sounds like it would save you the most time or headache right now? Happy to walk through any of them in more detail or hop on a quick call to map it to how you currently work.

## Notes

- Crafted to be realistic, specific, and pilot-friendly
- No AI jargon — pitched as a tool they control
- Each example maps to a known pain point for conference producers
- The gbrain example doubles as a subtle demo of the product in use

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

pages.title: Vps Hardening
pages.body:
# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­


It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered
(and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.
The annual goal trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on.

"Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing
happens.

By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything
that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for
next year.

I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I
set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine
on paper.

Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week
Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They
operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline
is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

Btw, this book is available on Shopee!

I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March.

I hit it!!

The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I
built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.
Why this works for your career too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot.

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target
department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to
leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One of these is a wish. The other is a plan.

I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal
like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached,
mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year"
mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it.
How to set yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Follow this format and write down:

   - *My 90-day career goal is...*
   - *This matters to me because...*
   - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...*
   (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
   - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the
   outcome)

Your homework this weekend

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey
area.

Then build it into your week, not your year.
------------------------------

P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3
2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in
June. For more info or to sign up, head over to

# The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca58bcdb09c70
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to Zaim's workspace via self-BCC

Zaim shared this during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

**Client:** KSI Senior Analyst → Product Manager
**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

Role: Senior Brand Manager at Unilever Malaysia
Context: Referred by Liyana from the KSI workshop last month. First session next Tuesday.

Key points from our intro call:
- 8 years in FMCG brand management, feeling stagnant
- Wants to pivot into tech but unsure where to start
- Main blocker: 'I don't have a technical background'
- Has actually done more data-driven work than she realises — ran consumer analytics for 3 product launches
- Salary anchor: RM 18k/month, worried about resetting to junior level

My read: Classic mid-career pivot case. The real problem isn't skills — it's confidence and narrative. She needs help reframing 'brand manager' as 'growth strategist who uses data.' Tech companies would kill for someone who can bridge consumer insights and product.

Plan for session 1: Audit her last 2 years of work through a tech lens. Find 3-5 specific examples where she already did 'product thinking' without calling it that.

— Zaim
